narration.default: A 1D array is a single ROW of slots — last chapter's scores[5] was 1D. A 2D array is a GRID — rows AND columns. Think of a tic-tac-toe board (3 rows × 3 columns), a chessboard (8 × 8), a Sudoku puzzle (9 × 9), pixels in a small picture (whatever × whatever), a school timetable (5 days × 8 hours). Anywhere you have a regular grid of values, a 2D array is the natural fit. Today: a simple 3×3 grid filled with numbers 1 to 9, walked through with two nested for loops.

Lines 4 to 8: int grid[3][3] = { {1,2,3}, {4,5,6}, {7,8,9} };. The shape: int NAME[ROWS][COLS] = { {row0}, {row1}, {row2} };. The TWO sets of brackets — [3][3] — say "3 rows, 3 columns". The init values look like a list of lists, each inner { } being one row. Reading line by line: row 0 is {1,2,3}, row 1 is {4,5,6}, row 2 is {7,8,9}. So the grid has 9 cells total (3 × 3 = 9).

To access a cell: grid[row][col]. grid[0][0] is top-left (= 1). grid[2][2] is bottom-right (= 9). grid[1][1] is the center (= 5). grid[1][2] is middle row, third column (= 6). The convention is ALWAYS [row][col] — outer index first (which row), inner index second (which column within that row). Don't mix them up — grid[2][1] (third row, second column = 8) is different from grid[1][2] (second row, third column = 6).

steps.title: Nested loops to walk the grid

narration.default: Lines 9 to 14: outer for runs rows 0 to 2, inner for runs cols 0 to 2 within each row. Inside the inner body: printf("%d ", grid[row][col]); — print the current cell with a space after it. After the inner loop finishes one row, line 13 prints \n to drop to the next line. Same nested-loops pattern as the chapter on nested loops, now with the grid index using both counters.

You don't always need a loop. Try adding (after the existing nested loops) a single line: printf("Center: %d\n", grid[1][1]); — that just prints 5 (the center cell). You can use grid[row][col] anywhere a normal int would go: in math (grid[0][0] + grid[2][2]), in conditions (if (grid[1][1] == 5)), in function arguments. The bracket syntax is your tool for digging into ANY specific cell whenever you need it.

Click Run. Console:
1 2 3
4 5 6
7 8 9
Nine numbers in a tidy square. The nested loop printed each cell, and the printf("\n") after each row dropped to the next line. The output is a real 2D layout. Without the newline, all 9 cells would land on one long line.

Tic-tac-toe (3×3 of chars: 'X', 'O', or ' '), Sudoku (9×9 of ints 0-9), a small picture (rows × cols of pixel colors), a school timetable (days × periods), a dungeon map (rows × cols of room types). Anywhere you have a grid of regular cells, 2D arrays are the natural fit. The nested for-loop pattern walks every cell. You now have the foundation for any grid-based program.
